products.title: Biota Palau "Firecracker" Acropora Mini-colony Grade A
products.body_html:
Acropora sp - Aquacultured Acropora varieties now available from Biota Palau! These are F2 frags from established F1 broodstock colonies.

The Biota Palau Firecracker Acropora is an eye-catching new aquacultured coral that adds an explosion of color and energy to any reef aquarium. With its vibrant purple base and electric green branch tips and polyps, the Firecracker Acropora is an instant standout in both daylight and under blue LED lighting.

Each mini-colony is unique, with excellent coloration and healthy growth already established. Experience the best of Palau’s reefs while supporting sustainability with the Biota Palau Firecracker Acropora.

Acropora are considered to be by some the pinnacle of reefkeeping. They're generally considered to be difficult to keep, but very rewarding. They need moderate to high lighting and moderate to high water flow. It's necessary to maintain stable alkalinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace elements such as potassium. These fragments are grown under EcoTech Radion and Philips Coral Care lights. We use and recommend Easy Reefs Easy Booster and Easy SPS coral food.

products.title: Clarkii Clownfish Proaquatix (Friday Delivery)
products.body_html:
Amphiprion clarkii- The Proaquatix Clarkii Clownfish now available through Biota is a wonderfully variable and hardy captive-bred version of Amphiprion clarkii. Each fish displays its own unique combination of body color, caudal fin color, and stripe count, ranging from yellow, tan, and black bodies to white or yellow tails and two or three white bars that may sometimes appear faint. This natural variation makes every individual a standout in the aquarium.

Though Clarkii clownfish will readily pair with common anemone species when available, they do not require an anemone to thrive. In the wild they form symbiotic relationships with pizza anemones (Cryptodendrum adhesivum), bubble-tip anemones (Entacmaea quadricolor), sand anemones (Heteractis aurora), Sebae anemones (Heteractis crispa), magnificent anemones (Heteractis magnifica), Malu anemones (Heteractis malu), long-tentacle anemones (Macrodactyla doreensis), and carpet anemones such as Stichodactyla gigantea, S. haddoni, and S. mertensii. In a home reef they will often adopt corals and rockwork as a substitute host.

Raised by Proaquatix, these Clarkii arrive tank-ready and free from the stress of wild collection. They have been conditioned to accept high-quality pellets and flakes as well as frozen Mysis shrimp, ocean plankton, and brine shrimp. Their captive-bred origin makes them more adaptable than wild specimens, so you can expect them to settle in quickly and feed eagerly.

Like all clownfish, Clarkii are sequential hermaphrodites: the largest fish becomes female and the next largest becomes male, establishing a stable social hierarchy. They are reef-safe and show little interest in corals or invertebrates, adding lively personality and bold patterning to any marine aquarium. Now available at Biota, the Proaquatix Clarkii Clownfish brings both spectacular color variation and sustainable provenance to your reef.

Please choose a Friday delivery

products.title: Cinnamon Clownfish Proaquatix (Friday Delivery)
products.body_html:
Amphiprion melanopus - The Proaquatix Cinnamon Clownfish is immediately recognizable by its deep red body, a single clean white stripe running through the operculum, and a characteristic black patch on its posterior flank. Some individuals may even display subtle reflective blue pigments within the white stripe, while the pelvic fins remain solid black, creating a striking contrast that enhances any reef display.

Raised entirely in aquaculture, these Cinnamon Clownfish arrive tank-ready and accustomed to prepared diets, making them hardier and more adaptable than wild-caught specimens. Although they naturally associate with bubble-tip anemones (Entacmaea quadricolor), Sebae anemones (Heteractis crispa), and magnificent anemones (Heteractis magnifica) in the wild, Proaquatix-bred specimens will thrive without a host but will readily accept one if provided.

Like all clownfish, these Cinnamon Clownfish are sequential hermaphrodites. In a group or pair, the largest fish becomes female and the next largest becomes male. They are reef-safe and generally ignore corals and invertebrates, often playfully adopting rockwork, algae, or even non-stinging corals as surrogate homes.

These captive-bred Cinnamon Clownfish have been weaned onto high-quality pellets and flakes, and eagerly accept frozen foods such as ocean plankton, mysis shrimp, and brine shrimp. Their robust health, striking coloration, and engaging behavior make them a superb choice for both seasoned reef keepers and hobbyists looking to add reliable performance and lasting beauty to their aquariums.

products.title: Bicinctus Clownfish Proaquatix (Friday Delivery)
products.body_html:
Amphiprion bicinctus - The Proaquatix captive bred Bicinctus Clownfish is distinguished by its vibrant yellow-orange body and two clean white stripes, one encircling the head and operculum and the other running vertically at mid-body. Each fish also has a larger, flowing tail and dorsal fin that accentuate its graceful swimming style.

In the wild this species inhabits the waters of the Red Sea where it forms a symbiotic relationship with sea anemones such as the Bubble Tip Anemone (Entacmaea quadricolor) and the Sebae Anemone (Heteractis crispa). Although Proaquatix-bred Bicinctus do not require anemone hosts in captivity they will readily accept them if present, often adopting nearby corals and rockwork as surrogate homes.

Like all clownfish Bicinctus are sequential hermaphrodites in which the largest fish becomes female and the next largest becomes male. Their social hierarchy is important to establish in pairs or small groups and the presence of the dominant female influences the growth and behavior of the male.

These captive-bred Bicinctus arrive tank-ready and have been trained to accept prepared diets including high-quality pellets and flakes as well as freshly frozen invertebrates such as mysis shrimp and ocean plankton. Their aquacultured origins make them hardier and easier to acclimate than wild-caught specimens. Reef-safe and generally reef-friendly they add dramatic color and personality to any marine aquarium while supporting sustainable aquaculture practices.

products.title: White Stripe Maroon Clownfish Proaquatix (Friday Delivery)
products.body_html:
Premnas biaculeatus - The White Stripe Maroon Clownfish is a stunning and bold addition to any saltwater aquarium. With a deep maroon body and crisp white stripes, this species is known for its striking contrast and commanding presence. These aquacultured specimens from Proaquatix are well adapted to life in captivity, offering aquarists a hardy alternative to wild-caught fish.

Maroon Clownfish are known for their strong personalities and can be more territorial than other clownfish species, especially as they mature. It's recommended to keep only one per tank unless you are an experienced hobbyist looking to establish a bonded pair. Even then, tank size and careful observation are important to ensure compatibility.

In the wild, White Stripe Maroon Clownfish are commonly found hosting bubble-tip anemones (Entacmaea quadricolor), and they readily form the same symbiotic relationships in aquariums if an anemone is available. However, a host anemone is not a requirement for their health or well-being in captivity.

These fish make a dynamic display in reef or fish-only tanks and are an excellent choice for aquarists looking for a bold, aquacultured centerpiece.

When combining Proaquatix with Biota fish in the same order, please choose a Friday delivery

products.title: Gold Stripe Maroon Clownfish Proaquatix (Friday Delivery)
products.body_html:
Premnas biaculeatus - The Gold Stripe Maroon Clownfish is one of the most eye-catching clownfish varieties available in the hobby. Known for its bold maroon coloration and striking golden-yellow stripes, this species makes a dramatic and beautiful addition to reef and fish-only aquariums. These Gold Stripe Maroons from Proaquatix are aquacultured, making them hardier and better adapted to aquarium life than their wild counterparts.

Gold Stripe Maroon Clownfish are known for their unique personality. While they can be territorial as they mature, especially during spawning, they are also intelligent and interactive with their environment and aquarists. Their distinctive coloration becomes more vibrant with age, as the white juvenile stripes gradually shift to gold over time.

In the wild, this species naturally hosts in bubble-tip anemones (Entacmaea quadricolor), and they will often form the same relationship in a captive setting if an anemone is present. However, a host anemone is not required for their care, and they will thrive in a well-maintained reef tank without one.

These fish do best in tanks of at least 30 gallons and should be housed with caution if pairing, as aggression can occur between individuals. Aquacultured by Proaquatix, this is a sustainable and aquarium-ready option for hobbyists looking for a show-stopping clownfish with a bit of attitude.

When combining Proaquatix with Biota fish in the same order, please choose a Friday delivery

products.title: Tomato Clownfish Proaquatix (Friday Delivery)
products.body_html:
Amphiprion frenatus - The Tomato Clownfish is a bold and colorful species known for its deep reddish-orange body and distinctive single white head bar. Bred by Proaquatix in the USA, these aquacultured clownfish are well-suited for home aquariums thanks to their hardy nature and adaptability. Tomato Clownfish are slightly more territorial than other species, making them best suited for medium to large tanks, especially when kept as a pair.

In the wild, Tomato Clownfish are most commonly associated with the Bubble Tip Anemone (Entacmaea quadricolor). In aquariums, they readily accept this host but don't require an anemone to thrive. They may also adopt other anemones, coral, algae, or rockwork as a home when an anemone is not present.

Like all aquacultured fish, Proaquatix Tomato Clownfish are raised on prepared foods, accustomed to aquarium life, and more sustainable than wild-caught alternatives. By choosing these captive-bred fish, you’re helping reduce pressure on reef ecosystems while adding a vibrant and resilient species to your reef.

When combining Proaquatix with Biota fish in the same order, please choose a Friday delivery

products.title: True Percula Clownfish Proaquatix (Friday Delivery)
products.body_html:
Amphiprion percula - The True Percula Clownfish is a classic and beloved species in the marine aquarium hobby. Known for its bright orange coloration, bold black margins, and three crisp white bands, this clownfish is often considered the most iconic of the group. Bred by Proaquatix in Florida, these aquacultured True Perculas are raised on prepared foods and acclimated to aquarium life from the start, making them a hardy and sustainable choice for reef keepers.

True Percula Clownfish naturally host in a variety of anemone species in the wild, including Carpet Anemones (Stichodactyla spp.), and Magnificent Sea Anemones (Heteractis magnifica). In captivity, they thrive with or without an anemone present, and may adopt other anemone species like Bubble Tip Anemones (E. quadricolor), corals, or rockwork as their home.

These fish are peaceful, small, and well-suited for small or large community reef aquariums. Choosing a captive-bred True Percula from Proaquatix means you’re supporting responsible aquaculture while enjoying a beautiful, hardy fish that adapts well to life in your tank.

products.title: Biota Aquariums Ceramic Mug - Colorful Fish Design (11oz)
products.body_html:
This vibrant Ceramic Mug is perfect for those who love to start their day with a splash of color. Featuring stunning aquatic designs, this mug brings a touch of the ocean to your morning routine. Its glossy finish and bright colors make each sip delightful, evoking feelings of joy and relaxation. Ideal for marine life enthusiasts, artists, and anyone who appreciates unique kitchenware. Perfect for gifting on occasions like birthdays, holidays, or special celebrations that call for something unique. Whether you’re enjoying coffee, tea, or your favorite hot beverage, this mug adds cheer to every cup.

All Biota marine life is 100% sustainably cultured. When you shop with Biota, you are ensuring long-term jobs in remote locations and directly contributing to reducing collection from the wild.

Biota is a world-leader in renewable aquaculture that gives back to the ocean and takes nothing from it. We have developed new, sustainable ways of culturing fish and other marine life for aquariums, food security, and conservation.

- Glossy ceramic finish for a sleek look
- Vibrant colors with stunning print quality
- Microwave-safe for easy heating
- Dishwasher-safe for convenient cleaning
- Available in two sizes: 11oz and 15oz

Care instructions

Ships separately directly from the printer for a low shipping rate.

products.title: Chicago Sunburst Anemone
products.body_html:
Entacmea quadricolor - The Chicago Sunburst Anemone is one of the most sought-after bubble tip anemones in the reef aquarium hobby. It is a strain of the Bubble Tip Anemone known for its exceptionally bright, vivid coloration, a striking combination of fiery orange, yellow, green, and deep red hues resembling a sunburst. While it’s not a separate species, its popularity comes from selective propagation and cultivation in captivity, making its coloration more consistent and intense than typical wild caught specimens. Chicago Sunburst Anemones often reproduce asexually by splitting, allowing reefers to keep this desirable morph going in captivity.

Like other bubble tip anemones, the Chicago Sunburst can move around the tank until it finds an ideal location with the right lighting and flow. It has a semi-aggressive nature and may sting nearby corals, so it should be given ample space. Hosting clownfish can be beneficial, as they often form symbiotic relationships, providing protection and interaction for both the anemone and fish.

This anemone thrives under moderate to high-intensity lighting and benefits from stable water parameters. A mature, well-established aquarium is recommended. While it can derive some nutrition from photosynthesis, occasional feedings of small meaty foods such as mysis shrimp or finely chopped seafood will support its health and growth.

Important Notes:

  • This anemone may split naturally through asexual reproduction, creating clones over time.
  • If it begins to move excessively, it may be searching for a better location due to lighting, flow, or water quality changes.
  • Always use anemone guards on wavemakers and pumps to prevent injury.
  • Avoid keeping this strain with other anemone strains or species.

Add a stunning burst of color to your reef tank with the Chicago Sunburst Anemone, a showpiece anemone that thrives in well-maintained systems.

products.title: Coca-Cola Candy Cane Coral
products.body_html:
Caulastrea - This Candycane coral is pastel mint green. These specimens are cultured and not taken from the reef. 

Candycane aka Trumpet corals need moderate lighting and water flow. It's necessary to maintain stable alkalinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace elements. Be sure to acclimate new coral to bright lighting slowly and carefully.

products.title: Green Rhodactis
products.body_html:
Biota Green Rhodactis mushrooms are easy to keep and a great choice for beginners stocking a newly matured aquarium. These specimens are cultured and not taken from the reef. 

Mushroom corals need low to moderate lighting and water flow. Be sure to acclimate new coral to bright lighting slowly and carefully.

products.title: Squamosina Clam
products.body_html:
Tridacna squamosina - The squamosina clam is sometimes confused with the similar squamosa clam. The Squamosa clam is a relatively new clam to the aquarium trade and is cultured in the Red Sea. It is easy to tell a Squamosina clam from other clams by its unique, bright lines running through the mantle.

This species does well in medium to high lighting and has large scutes protruding off of it’s shell. These clams are great beginner clams for aquarists since they grow quickly and are very hardy. Feeding on Easy Reefs Easy Booster

products.title: Biota Salmon Roe Montipora frag
products.body_html:
Montipora sp. - This beautiful and unique Montipora is cultured by Biota. This lovely Montipora is a salmon color with orange polyps. It is a fast growing, hardy Montipora, great for those who are new to SPS corals. 

The Montipora is a small polyp stony coral that prefers moderate to strong water flow and lighting. Like other SPS corals, this coral requires stable alkalinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace elements for health and skeleton building.

products.title: Biota Palau Chubby Pikachu Acropora frag
products.body_html:
Acropora sp. - Aquacultured Biota Chubby Pikachu Acropora frag

Acropora are considered to be by some the pinnacle of reefkeeping. They're generally considered to be difficult to keep, but very rewarding. They need moderate to high lighting and moderate to high water flow. It's necessary to maintain stable alkalinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace elements such as potassium. These fragments are grown under EcoTech Radion and Philips Coral Care lights. We use and recommend Easy Reefs Easy Booster and Easy SPS coral food.

products.title: Pink and Green Stylophora
products.body_html:
Stylophora - This SPS coral is hardier and easier to keep than the similar Acropora coral, but has similar elegant branching shape and multiple beautiful colors. These specimens are cultured and not taken from the reef.

Stylophora need moderate to high lighting and moderate to high water flow. It's necessary to maintain stable alkalinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace elements. Be sure to acclimate new coral to bright lighting slowly and carefully.

products.title: Spurilla Nudibranch - Aiptasia Eater
products.body_html:
Spurilla sp. - The Biota captive bred Spurilla Nudibranch is a type of Aeolid nudibranch. The species commonly associated with the name "Spurilla" in North Carolina waters is likely Spurilla neapolitana, a species widely distributed in warm temperate and tropical waters, though identification can sometimes be tricky due to the variety of similar nudibranchs.

We find that the Spurilla we breed at Biota North Carolina are voracious Aiptasia eaters. This is a larger nudibranch, ranging from 1 to 3 inches in length. 

Spurilla nudibranchs are typically characterized by their elongated bodies, covered with cerata (finger-like projections) that house extensions of their digestive system. These cerata may be orange, brown, or even translucent, depending on the species and their diet. Their coloration often reflects the types of prey they consume, mainly anemones and other cnidarians. The pigments from their diet can give them a striking appearance.

In nature this nudibranch is found in shallow coastal waters, including tide pools and rocky intertidal zones, Spurilla nudibranchs are often spotted along the coast of North Carolina. They are most commonly observed on or near their prey, such as sea anemones.

These nudibranchs are specialist feeders that consume cnidarians like anemones. Remarkably, they can store the stinging cells (nematocysts) from their prey within their cerata, using them for their own defense.

Like all nudibranchs, Spurilla are simultaneous hermaphrodites, meaning they have both male and female reproductive organs. After mating, they lay spiral egg ribbons that hatch into free-swimming larvae.

products.title: Yellowhead Jawfish
products.body_html:
Opistognathus aurifrons - The Yellowhead Jawfish, bred responsibly by MERS, is a burrowing fish with lots of personality. Known for their bright yellow heads and shimmering white bodies with blue iridescence, these adorable fish are as playful as they are beautiful.

Yellowhead Jawfish are fascinating additions to nano or larger aquariums, creating burrows in sandy substrates and popping their heads out to keep an eye on their surroundings. They thrive best in peaceful communities and are perfect for reef tanks. A tight fitting lid is necessary to prevent this species from jumping out of the aquarium. 

Minimum Tank Size: 30 gallons with plenty of fine sand and rubble for burrow building.

products.title: Harlequin Shrimp
products.body_html:
Hymenocera picta - The captive bred Harlequin Shrimp is a stunning invertebrate famous for their intricate polka-dot patterns and delicate movements. They feed on sea stars such as pest Asterina or Chocolate Chip Sea Stars.

Care Level: Moderate
Diet: Starfish/Sea Stars (Asterina or other species)
Reef Safe: Yes (except Sea Stars)
Maximum Size: ~2 inches

Harlequin Shrimp are natural starfish predators, known for their fascinating feeding habits. They thrive in mature reef systems with peaceful tankmates and are often kept in male/female pairs.

By choosing captive bred Harlequin Shrimp, you’ll enjoy healthier, more resilient animals while supporting sustainable aquaculture efforts that protect wild reef populations.

Minimum Tank Size: 20 gallons with plenty of hiding spots and a peaceful environment.

If you have larger fish in your tank, we recommend keeping them in an acclimation box for a time.

products.title: Forest Fire Montipora Frag
products.body_html:
Montipora digitata - This beautiful Forest Fire Montipora is a fast growing, hardy Montipora, great for those who are new to SPS corals.

The Montipora is a small polyp stony coral that prefers moderate to strong water flow and lighting. Like other SPS corals, this coral requires stable alkalinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace elements for health and skeleton building.

products.title: Tangerine Sea Fan
products.body_html:
Annella mollis or A. reticulata - The Biota Palau Tangerine Sea Fan has long, thin orange branches and polyps. Large colonies are impressive with their hypnotic movements. This species makes a great addition to reef tanks or seahorse tanks. This species is not common in the aquarium trade. These specimens are cultured at Biota Palau and not taken from the wild reefs. 

It is necessary to feed several times a day with microfoods designed for soft corals along with a wide variety of coral foods and phytoplankton. You can use a dosing pump to frequently provide meals for best results. Maintain stable water parameters, especially iodine and trace elements to keep this coral healthy.

products.title: Green Center Brain Coral
products.body_html:
Faviidae -  This Green Center "Brain Coral" is aquacultured at our Biota Palau facility. These are a type of large polyp stony coral that will benefit from stable alkalinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace elements in the water. It prefers moderate water flow and light, and it does well in the lower sections of well-lit tanks. Use care when acclimating it to your lighting. 

This hardy species is a good choice for beginners who have a stable environment and water parameters. This coral will quickly encrust over any rock or adjacent surface.

products.body_html:
Ricordea florida - Ricordea florida mushrooms are easy to keep and a good choice for beginners stocking a newly matured reef aquarium. These specimens are cultured and not taken from the reef. 

Mushroom corals need low to moderate lighting and water flow. Be sure to acclimate new coral to bright lighting slowly and carefully.

products.title: White Urchin
products.body_html: Lytechinus variegatus - The White urchin is a beautiful and ravenous herbivore bred and raised at Biota North Carolina. These inverts make great additions to your clean up crew as they can eat both tough algae on rockwork and film algae on the glass. They tend to pick up small rocks or sand around the aquarium so make sure your frags are glued down.

products.title: Cultured Black Dragon Soul Torch
products.body_html: Euphyllia glabrescens - Prepare for unparalleled drama in your reef with the cultured Ultra Black Dragon Soul Torch Coral. This striking torch coral is as mesmerizing as it is rare, featuring a deep, almost-black base that transitions to vibrant green and yellow tips. Its long, flowing tentacles dance gracefully with the current, creating a hypnotic movement that captures attention from across the room.

products.title: Cultured Holy Grail Torch frag
products.body_html: Euphyllia glabrescens - Introducing the ultimate show stopper for any reef aquarium - the Holy Grail Torch Coral Frag. Biota staff says this variety is EPIC in person with its neon green base and pink tips, this Holy Grail Torch is a true masterpiece. Each frag glows intensely under actinic reef lighting, creating a mesmerizing effect that transforms your reef into a piece of living art. This coral is sought after for its rare, electric colors and striking tentacle movement, making it a centerpiece for the most discerning reefers.

products.title: Cultured Gold Torch frag
products.body_html: Euphyllia glabrescens - Add a warm, golden glow to your reef with the Gold Torch Coral Frag. This variety has warm toned tentacles with a golden dusting. Known for its elegant, flowing tentacles, this coral adds movement and charm to your reef tank.

products.title: Cultured 24K Gold Torch colony
products.body_html: Euphyllia glabrescens - Bring a touch of luxury to your reef tank with the dazzling 24K Gold Torch Coral. Known for its radiant, golden-yellow tentacles and shimmering tips, this cultured frag brings the beauty and brilliance of rare, high-end coral to your tank. The 24K Gold Torch Coral creates a striking centerpiece with its flowing tentacles adding movement and depth to your aquascape.

products.title: Cultured B grade Holy Grail Torch frag
products.body_html: Euphyllia glabrescens - B grade Holy Grail Torch frag - a great option for those looking to enjoy the beauty of the Holy Grail Torch without the premium price, this B-Grade frag is a budget-friendly way to elevate your coral collection. Ideal for both beginner and experienced aquarists.

products.title: Minty Green Toadstool Coral
products.body_html:
Toadstool corals are fast growing and hardy, easy to keep soft corals and a great choice for beginners stocking a newly matured reef aquarium. These specimens were cultured and not taken from the reef.

Toadstool corals need moderate to high lighting and water flow. Be sure to acclimate new coral to bright lighting slowly and carefully.

products.title: Springeri Dottyback
products.body_html:
Pseudochromis springeri - The Springeri Dottyback is a stunning fish with a black body and neon blue "racing stripes" on the face and head. This species is very hardy and easy to care for. 

Springeri Dottybacks are valuable in reef tanks for their penchant for eating small bristleworms. Over time, they have been known to reduce numbers of pesky bristleworms in home aquariums that are overrun. Dottybacks will eat most popular aquarium foods including a variety of frozen foods and Easy Reefs DKI pellets and Masstick. This Dottyback is semi-aggressive and requires a 30 gallon aquarium when full grown.

products.title: Yellow Trimma Goby
products.body_html: Trimma kitrinum - The Yellow Trimma goby is a bright star in the nano goby family. Sporting a yellow body these gobies dart from rock work to rock work like flashes of lightning. These fish do great either singly or in small groups and grow to around 1” in sizing so they’re great for nano set ups and larger aquariums.

products.title: Jewel Urchin
products.body_html:
Lytechinus williamsi  - The Jewel Urchin is a great clean-up crew critter for reef aquariums, known for its compact size and peaceful nature. They can eat both tough algae on rockwork and film algae on the glass. 

As a herbivorous grazer, the Jewel Urchin plays an essential role in controlling algae growth. This captive bred urchin is sustainably sourced and accustomed to aquarium life, making it a hardy and eco-friendly choice for your setup. They tend to pick up small rocks or sand around the aquarium so make sure your frags are glued down.

products.title: "Mini Trochus" Collonista Snails
products.body_html:
Collonista spp. - The Mini Trochus Snail is a ravenous herbivore bred and raised at Biota Florida. These inverts make great additions to your clean up crew as they eat film algae on your glass and even some Cyanobacteria and Diatoms. These snails are extremely tiny even as adults. They're perfect for nano or pico tanks, but they're also great for large tanks because they can fit into crevices in rocks and places larger snails can't reach. 

These snails will reproduce in your tank, unlike the majority of other snails. This species is incredibly hardy and forgiving. 

These snails are small enough to fit in pumps and plumbing, but with your regular cleaning they're manageable.

products.title: Maxima Clam Red Sea
products.body_html:
Tridacna maxima - The Maxima clam also known as the small giant clam is the most popular giant clam species in the aquarium trade. Maxima clams have some of the brightest coloration of any of the giant clam species. They do very well in aquariums with higher lighting and should be kept in the rock work so they can attach with their byssal threads. They can reach a max size of 12”. 

Type A is similar to Grade A/Ultra clams with more blue and purple coloration

Type B will have more Greens and browns

products.title: Brazillian Gramma Basslet
products.body_html:
Gramma brasiliensis - The Brazilian Gramma Basslet is a close relative of the Royal Gramma Basslet, but much more rare in the aquarium industry. They're very similar to G. loreto, but the yellow to purple transition on the body of G. brasiliensis is less sharp and wider than in G. loreto. It also has a slightly different head shape with a wider mouth than G. loreto. Since G. brasiliensis is no longer offered as wild caught to the aquarium trade, only captive bred specimens are available.

Gramma basslets are undisputedly some of the most perfect reef tank citizens. These fish are extremely popular because they're not only gorgeous with their bright complementary colors, but they're hardy, robust, and easy even for beginners to keep and feed. Unlike many other small fish, they're not as timid after the acclimation period and tend to spend time in the water column entertaining their humans. They are peaceful toward even the most timid fish like seahorses. They're completely reef safe with all inverts and corals (except maybe hitchhiking amphipods that grow in most tanks).

This species does well in nano tanks, but they're gregarious enough for large tanks. The ideal tank size is 30+ gallons. They can be kept singly or in small groups if added at the same time.

Brazilian Gramma Basslets are not picky when it comes to feeding and will take most aquarium foods. These captive bred fish are currently feeding on C1, PE pellet, and Hikari Mysis Shrimp. We recommend a varied diet of frozen meaty foods and Easy Reefs DKI pellets and Masstick.

Basslets are shipped inside a mesh tube. Please do not remove the fish from the tube; allow the fish to come out of the tube on its own. It's best to add the tube with the fish inside to the tank. We recommend introducing new fish with an acclimation box.

Small: ~ 1"+ 

Photo credit: Daniel Filgueiras - https://www.flickr.com/photos/drfilgueiras/4295695155/

products.title: Yellow Neon Cleaner Goby
products.body_html:
Elacatinus figaro - The captive bred Yellow Neon Cleaner Goby is very similar to the popular Blue Neon Cleaner Goby. They are small, hardy goby perfect for nano aquariums.  This species is a facultative cleaner that will gently clean larger fish in your aquarium. They do not rely on cleaning large fish as a food source and will readily accept a wide range of foods like tiny pellet foods, frozen calanus, Hikari baby brine shrimp, and small chopped mysis. They can also live in nano tanks or species only tanks without fish to clean.

Neon gobies are difficult to sex reliably. It's best to keep a single goby or a mated pair. Larger aquariums with plenty of rock work for territories may be able to support larger groups of these, but they can be expected to quarrel, and a larger dominant male can be very aggressive with other Neon Gobies. These are peaceful toward non-conspecifics and completely reef safe with all coral and ornamental inverts. Most larger species will recognize them as cleaners, but it's best to avoid keeping these with aggressive species. It's possible to keep different species of Elacatinus together in larger aquariums. We recommend introducing new fish with an acclimation box.

Captive bred Neon Gobies tend to be much hardier than their wild caught counterparts, which are often suffering with parasites when they arrive at your local aquarium shop. This species is short-lived, so choosing captive bred Neon Gobies is a wise choice so you know you're receiving a young specimen.

products.title: Shortfin Pipefish
products.body_html:
Cosmocampus elucens - The Shortfin Pipefish is a hardy pipefish species native to the seagrass beds of the Western Atlantic. This species is very similar to their seahorse cousins in appearance and behavior, and they make excellent seahorse tank mates. This species is highly variable in coloration, and they can even camouflage to match their surroundings.

Shortfin Pipefish are a demersal variety of pipefish, spending most of their time meandering around on the substrate and rocks. During feeding time, however, they become very lively and will free-swim in the water column hunting down frozen mysis shrimp. Pipefish are playful and socially engaging animals that make unique and wonderful pets. 

Wild caught pipefish are extremely difficult to keep because they typically only eat live foods. Captive bred pipefish are raised on prepared foods, making them much easier to keep. They're quite hardy when provided with the proper seahorse-specific environment and care. Biota's captive bred pipefish are healthy and robust, being bred and raised in Biota's North Carolina facility by marine biologists with decades of seahorse experience.

Feeding

Pipefish should be fed a staple diet of frozen Mysis shrimp along with occasional feedings of other small, meaty foods like frozen Spirulina enriched brine shrimp and large species of copepods like frozen Calanus copepods. Young pipefish should be fed 3 - 4 x a day, and fully grown adults at least twice a day. 

Compatibility

Young pipefish should ideally be kept in seahorse & pipefish-only tanks or with only the smallest, most peaceful fish species like our nano gobies. As they grow and mature, other appropriate peaceful species may be added to their aquarium. Choose slow moving tank mates that won't outcompete the pipefish for food. Basslets, Cardinalfish, Filefish, and similar species can make good tank mates for full grown, acclimated adults.

Pipefish are slow and easily injured by other life forms in the tank. Very small hermit crabs are safe, but do not keep them with larger crabs and hermit crabs or any animals that could pinch or harm them physically. Stinging anemones and any stinging corals like Euphyllia are not suitable tank mates for demersal pipefish.

Soft corals like mushrooms, Zoanthus, Toadstool, Sinularia, Tree corals, Xenia, Green Star Polyps, and gorgonians are excellent additions to a pipefish tank. 

This species prefers cooler waters up to 74°F. A heater may be provided for stability, but make sure the heater is covered or placed in a sump, as pipefish can be burned by wrapping themselves around the heater. Adults can reach up to 6" long. A 20 gallon aquarium is suitable for up to four young pipefish. 

Seahorses and pipefish are very different from other aquarium fish, and their care and requirements are also unique to them. Feel free to reach out to us with any questions at support@thebiotagroup.com

products.title: Yellow Heraldi Angelfish
products.body_html:
Centropyge heraldi - The Yellow Heraldi Angelfish or "False Lemonpeel Angelfish" is a dwarf angelfish species bred at our Biota Palau facility. It is bright yellow in coloration with faint orange markings around the eyes and fins. Some individuals have black edges on the fins, but this variety is entirely yellow. One of the larger Centropyge angelfish, this species grows to an impressive but manageable 4". 

They are a "reef safe with caution" species so be sure to keep them fed on a wide variety of feeds that include Easy Reefs DKI pellets, Spirulina brine shrimp, frozen Calanus, and Angelfish specific preparations. Use caution when adding to a tank of other dwarf angelfish or similar looking species. We always recommend adding new fish with an acclimation box.

products.title: Biota Mystery Machine Goniopora frag
products.body_html:
Goniopora sp. - This incredibly colored Flowerpot coral has a blue mouth surrounded by neon green tentacles. These specimens are cultured, not taken from the reef. 

Goniopora corals need moderate lighting and water flow. It's necessary to maintain stable alkalinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace elements. Be sure to acclimate new coral to bright lighting slowly and carefully. This species requires regular feedings of appropriate coral foods.

products.title: Biota Palau Elilai Montipora Frag
products.body_html:
Montipora sp. - This beautiful and unique Montipora is cultured at the Biota Palau facility. This coral has a neon green rim and purple polyps. It is a fast growing, hardy Montipora, great for those who are new to SPS corals.

The Elilai Montipora gets its name from the beautiful Plumeria flower. Known as "Elilai" in Palau, this national flower is an important part of Palauan culture. 

The Montipora is a small polyp stony coral that prefers moderate to strong water flow and lighting. Like other SPS corals, this coral requires stable alkalinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace elements for health and skeleton building.

products.title: Splatter Hammer Coral
products.body_html:
Fimbriaphyllia paraancora - These Splatter Hammer coral frags are growing in our Biota Florida facility. The mother colony was cultured and not taken from the reef. This strain was chosen for its incredible neon green and purple "splatter" coloration and its interesting branchy growth pattern. 

Hammer corals need moderate lighting and moderate water flow. It's necessary to maintain stable alkalinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace elements. Be sure to acclimate new coral to bright lighting slowly and carefully.

products.title: Yellow/Green Wall Hammer colony
products.body_html:
Euphyllia - This variety of hammer coral has a yellow-green coloration and a lot of fluorescence under actinic lighting. These specimens are cultured and not taken from the reef. 

Give this stinging species plenty of space to grow away from other corals. 

Hammer corals need moderate lighting and water flow. It's necessary to maintain stable alkalinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace elements. Be sure to acclimate new coral to bright lighting slowly and carefully.

products.title: Biota Palau Pocillopora
products.body_html:
Pocillopora sp. - These Pocillopora are sexually reproduced in captivity at our Biota Palau facility. This species is very hardy for a small polyp stony coral and is a great beginner coral for aquarists wanting to get started with SPS corals.

Pocillopora need moderate to high lighting and moderate to high water flow. It's necessary to maintain stable alkalinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace elements such as potassium. These fragments are grown under EcoTech Radion and Philips Coral Care lights. We use and recommend Easy Reefs Easy Booster and Easy SPS coral food.

products.title: Biota Palau Leptastrea Coral assorted
products.body_html:
Leptastrea sp. - This purple and neon green Leptastrea Coral is farmed in Palau. 

Leptastrea is a type of large polyp stony coral that will benefit from stable alkalinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace elements in the water. It prefers moderate water flow and light, and it does well in the lower sections of well-lit tanks. Use care when acclimating it to your lighting. 

This hardy species is a good choice for beginners who have a stable environment and water parameters. This coral will quickly encrust over any rock or adjacent surface.

products.title: Assorted Palau Faviids
products.body_html:
These Faviid family corals are aquacultured at our Biota Palau facility. 

These are a type of large polyp stony coral that will benefit from stable alkalinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace elements in the water. It prefers moderate water flow and light, and it does well in the lower sections of well-lit tanks. Use care when acclimating it to your lighting. 

This hardy species is a good choice for beginners who have a stable environment and water parameters. This coral will quickly encrust over any rock or adjacent surface.

Please note the photo shows the mother colony. Frags are 1 or 2 heads

products.title: Biota green Montipora Cap Frag
products.body_html:
Montipora sp. - This red/pink "Monti Cap" has been growing in our Biota Florida facility for some time, and we are now offering in-house grown frags. 

The Montipora "cap" is a small polyp stony coral that prefers moderate to strong water flow and lighting. It is a scrolling/plating species that will form elegant whorls and plates. It is a fast grower and one of the hardier SPS corals.

Like other SPS corals, this coral requires stable alkalinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace elements for health and skeleton building.

products.title: Rhodactis Mushroom assorted
products.body_html:

Mushroom corals need low to moderate lighting and water flow. Be sure to acclimate new coral to bright lighting slowly and carefully.

products.title: Yellow Mopsella Sea Fan
products.body_html:
Mopsella sp. - The Biota Palau Yellow Mopsella Sea Fan has long, thin yellow branches with red spots and white polyps. Large colonies are impressive with their hypnotic movements. This species makes a great addition to reef tanks or seahorse tanks. This species is not common in the aquarium trade. These specimens are cultured at Biota Palau and not taken from the wild reefs. 

Mopsella is nonphotosynthetic and difficult to keep in captivity for all but experienced sea fan and NPS coral keeprs. Be sure to place this coral in a shady area with moderate laminar flow to avoid algae growth on the coral. It is necessary to feed several times a day with microfoods designed for soft corals along with a wide variety of coral foods and phytoplankton. You can use a dosing pump to frequently provide meals for best results. Maintain stable water parameters, especially iodine and trace elements to keep this coral healthy.

products.title: Red Swiftia Sea Fan
products.body_html:
Swiftia exserta - The Biota Palau Red Swiftia Sea Fan has long, knobby red branches with white polyps. Large colonies are impressive with their hypnotic movements and many fuzzy branches. This species makes a great addition to reef tanks or seahorse tanks. Though this species isn't common in the wild, it's popular in the aquarium trade because of its amazing color. These specimens are cultured at Biota Palau and not taken from the wild reefs. 

This species is nonphotosynthetic and difficult to keep in captivity, but is considered to be one of the easier to keep NPS Gorgonians. Be sure to place this coral in a shady area with moderate laminar flow to avoid algae growth on the coral. It is necessary to feed several times a day with microfoods designed for soft corals along with a wide variety of coral foods and phytoplankton. You can use a dosing pump to frequently provide meals for best results. Maintain stable water parameters, especially iodine and trace elements to keep this coral healthy.

products.title: Aiptasia Solution Pack
products.body_html:
Save $20 when you purchase the pack!

Aiptasia Solution Pack includes:

  • 1 small Aiptasia Eating Filefish 
  • 3 Fancy Peppermint shrimp


Acreichthys tomentosus - Aiptasia Eating Filefish aka Matted Filefish are an incredibly helpful species of filefish. They are called the Aiptasia Eating Filefish because of their propensity to eat nuisance anemones which plague most home aquariums like Aiptasia, majano anemones, and some types of hydroids. They do not require Aiptasia and will eat most aquarium foods. These interesting little filefish have the ability to change coloration and blend in with their surroundings rapidly, making it fun to watch them roam around the tank.

This species is reef safe with caution, as some individuals may nip at soft corals, zoas, SPS polyps, fleshy LPS corals, clam mantles, and/or tiny ornamental shrimp. They do well in tanks 30 gallons or larger and are peaceful toward other fish species. They are territorial with their own kind, but can be kept in pairs. It's easy to sex these fish as adults because the males have clear bristles on the caudal peduncle near the base of the tail, giving this fish its other common name of Bristletail Filefish. 

Omnivorous Aiptasia Eating Filefish will eat most popular aquarium foods including a variety of frozen foods and Easy Reefs DKI pellets and Masstick. Feeding 0.8mm DKI pellets, Reef nutrition C1, C2, Hikari Mysis, PE Calanus. Current size: approximately 1" please allow for variation in size.

This species is naturally a poor swimmer, so ensure their safety by covering wavemakers/overflows and make sure the flow isn't too fast for them. We recommend using an acclimation box to introduce them to the new tank. Use caution when housing these with anemones or other corals with a strong sting.

You can show your support for Biota by adding a T shirt, sticker, mug, or other Biota merch to your order today!


Lysmata jundalini - Fancy Peppermint Shrimp are feeding on Aiptasia and PE Pellets. Biota recommends introducing new livestock using an acclimation box. 

Bocas Mariculture founder Till Deuss spoke with journalist Matthew Pedersen about his recent success breeding this new peppermint shrimp species -

"First, there are its unique, pleasing aesthetics. 'The species is quite a bit more attractive than other species of peppermint shrimps, having golden legs, a conspicuous blue spot on the coxa between the pereiopods, and blue shimmer on the antennular flagellums.'

These small shrimps will be sought out for their pest-control abilities. Says Deuss, 'I have kept and cultured several other species of peppermint shrimps in the past, and in my experience L. jundalini seems to be particularly efficient in controlling problematic Aiptasia anemones.'"

Excerpt from "Lysmata jundalini: a captive-bred first from Bocas Mariculture" Reef2Rainforest Media https://www.reef2rainforest.com/2020/12/11/lysmata-jundalini-a-captive-bred-first-from-bocas-mariculture/

products.title: Gold Torch Coral
products.body_html:
Euphyllia glabrescens- This Gold Torch Coral is cultured and not taken from the reef. 

Give plenty of space for this stinging coral to grow away from other species. Torch corals need moderate lighting and moderate water flow. It's necessary to maintain stable alkalinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace elements. Be sure to acclimate new coral to bright lighting slowly and carefully. 

Please note the photo shows the mother colony. Frags are 1 or 2 heads

products.title: Neon Green Toadstool Coral
products.body_html:
Toadstool corals are fast growing and hardy, easy to keep soft corals and a great choice for beginners stocking a newly matured reef aquarium. These specimens were cultured and not taken from the reef.

Toadstool corals need moderate to high lighting and water flow. Be sure to acclimate new coral to bright lighting slowly and carefully.

products.title: Hologram Hammer Coral
products.body_html:
Euphyllia sp. - The Hologram Hammer coral is cultured and not taken from the reef. This strain was chosen for its incredible coloration. Biota Jake says they remind him of a holographic Pokémon card. This is a truly unique and beautiful Hammer coral. 

Hammer corals need moderate lighting and moderate water flow. It's necessary to maintain stable alkalinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace elements. Be sure to acclimate new coral to bright lighting slowly and carefully.

products.title: Biota Branching Galaxea
products.body_html:
Galaxea horrescens or Acrohelia horrescens - This interesting and uncommon branching Galaxea is farmed at Biota. We haven't seen many of these in the aquarium hobby, so this is a great piece for the collector of unusual corals.

Unlike other Galaxea, this species tends to have shorter, less deadly sweeper tentacles. It's still wise to give it room to grow and keep it from touching other coral in your tank.

Galaxea is a type of large polyp stony coral that will benefit from stable alkalinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace elements in the water. It prefers moderate water flow and light, and it does well in the lower sections of well-lit tanks. Use care when acclimating it to your lighting. 

This hardy species is a good choice for beginners who have a stable environment and water parameters. This coral will quickly grow new branches.

products.title: Raccoon Butterflyfish
products.body_html:
Chaetodon lunula - The Biota captive bred Raccoon Butterflyfish, Chaetodon lunula, is being produced at The Hawaii Pacific University - Oceanic Institute and Biota Hawaii. This is the first time Chaetodon lunula has been raised from eggs produced in captivity and offered commercially as a captive bred offering. 

The Raccoon Butterflyfish is a hardy, beautiful species with a bright yellow body and a black mask. This species when wild caught may not eat when first introduced to a new aquarium, but has a great appetite after acclimation. Our captive bred specimens are even hardier, better suited to aquarium life, and are raised on small pellets and other inexpensive aquarium foods; making them an excellent choice for beginner aquarists. They’ve also been known to enjoy eating pest Aiptasia anemones in aquariums, making them a valuable worker fish.

This medium-sized species reaches a max size of less than 8” in aquariums. As adults, they do best in a large fish-only aquarium with other peaceful species.

products.title: Clove Polyp Orange Vein
products.body_html:
Clavularia sp. Clove Polyps are easy to keep and a good choice for beginners stocking a newly matured reef aquarium. These specimens are cultured and not taken from the reef. 

Clove Polyps need low to moderate lighting and strong water flow. Be sure to acclimate new coral to bright lighting slowly and carefully.

products.title: Clove Polyp Orange Tip
products.body_html:
Clavularia sp. Clove Polyps are easy to keep and a good choice for beginners stocking a newly matured reef aquarium. These specimens are cultured and not taken from the reef. 

Clove Polyps need low to moderate lighting and strong water flow. Be sure to acclimate new coral to bright lighting slowly and carefully.

products.title: Clove Polyp Green
products.body_html:
Clavularia sp. Clove Polyps are easy to keep and a good choice for beginners stocking a newly matured reef aquarium. These specimens are cultured and not taken from the reef.

products.body_html:

Toadstool corals need moderate to high lighting and water flow. Be sure to acclimate new coral to bright lighting slowly and carefully.

products.title: Ricordea Yuma Mushroom - with stripes
products.body_html:
Ricordea yuma mushrooms are easy to keep and a good choice for beginners stocking a newly matured reef aquarium. These specimens are cultured and not taken from the reef. 

Mushroom corals need low to moderate lighting and water flow. Be sure to acclimate new coral to bright lighting slowly and carefully.

products.title: Ricordea Yuma Mushroom - Green
products.body_html:
Ricordea yuma mushrooms are easy to keep and a good choice for beginners stocking a newly matured reef aquarium. These specimens are cultured and not taken from the reef. 

Mushroom corals need low to moderate lighting and water flow. Be sure to acclimate new coral to bright lighting slowly and carefully.

products.title: Biota Palau Lobophyllia
products.body_html:
Lobophyllia sp. - This is Lobed brain coral is prized for its coloration. These specimens are farmed in Palau and not taken from the wild. 

Brain corals need moderate to high lighting and moderate water flow. It's necessary to maintain stable alkalinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace elements. Be sure to acclimate new coral to new lighting slowly and carefully.

products.title: Black Cap Basslet
products.body_html:
Gramma melacara - The Black Cap Basslet is closely related to the Royal Gramma Basslet, but is less commonly available for home aquariums. This genus is undisputedly one of the most perfect reef tank citizens. These fish are extremely popular because they're not only gorgeous with their bright colors, but they're hardy, robust, and easy even for beginners to keep and feed. Unlike many other small fish, they're not as timid after the acclimation period and tend to spend time in the water column entertaining their humans. They are peaceful toward even the most timid fish like seahorses, but they may be semi-aggressive with other basslets and similar species. They're completely reef safe with ornamental inverts and corals.

The ideal tank size is 30+ gallons. They can be kept singly or in small groups if added at the same time. 

Black Cap Basslets are not picky when it comes to feeding and will take most aquarium foods. These captive bred fish are currently feeding on tiny pellets and small Mysis Shrimp. We recommend a varied diet of frozen meaty foods and Easy Reefs DKI pellets and Masstick.

Gramma are shipped inside a mesh tube. Please do not remove the fish from the tube; allow the fish to come out of the tube on its own. It's best to add the tube with the fish inside to the tank. These fish are small on arrival. We strongly recommend introducing new fish with an acclimation box.

products.title: Bali Pink and Green Ball Goniopora
products.body_html:
Goniopora sp. - Bali Pink and Green Ball Goniopora have pink centers and green tentacles. 

These corals are called "Flowerpot Corals" for good reason. Long, flowing stalks with flower-like polyps at the ends extend from a ball-shaped skeleton. The polyps will be closed when it arrives, but be sure to give it plenty of room to expand, as the tentacles significantly stretch out of the base. 

Goniopora were at one time called "6 month corals" because many wild collected specimens don't do well in captivity. Thanks to aquaculture and the invention of coral foods, many reefers are able to keep this unique and beautiful genus.

This species prefers gentle, moderate water flow and low to medium light. Take care to slowly acclimate it to the lighting in your aquarium. We recommend regular feedings of micro coral foods like Goniopower, Coral Frenzy, and Reef Roids.

variants.title: Default Title
variants.sku: Goniopora sp. (Pink and Green Ball Goni)-R

products.title: Cabbage Leather Frag
products.body_html:
Sinularia brassica - This green Cabbage coral frags are aquacultured and not taken from the reef. This species is fast growing, easy to care for, and excellent for beginners.

Don't be alarmed if your Cabbage Coral closes up temporarily. They are known to shed the waxy outer coating to rid themselves of algae and benthic growths.

Cabbage corals need moderate lighting and good water flow. They attach to rock and do best in the bottom to mid region of the aquarium. Be sure to acclimate new coral to bright lighting slowly and carefully.

products.title: Ricordea Yuma Mushroom - Lime Green
products.body_html:
Ricordea yuma mushrooms are easy to keep and a good choice for beginners stocking a newly matured reef aquarium. These specimens are cultured and not taken from the reef. 

Mushroom corals need low to moderate lighting and water flow. Be sure to acclimate new coral to bright lighting slowly and carefully.

products.title: Ricordea Yuma Mushroom - 2 color
products.body_html:
Ricordea yuma mushrooms are easy to keep and a good choice for beginners stocking a newly matured reef aquarium. These specimens are cultured and not taken from the reef. 

Mushroom corals need low to moderate lighting and water flow. Be sure to acclimate new coral to bright lighting slowly and carefully.

products.title: Neon Dottyback (Proaquatix) Friday Delivery
products.body_html:
Pseudochromis aldabraensis - The Neon Dottyback is a stunning fish with a bright orange body and neon blue "racing stripes." This species is very hardy and easy to care for. 

This species is valuable in reef tanks for its penchant for eating small bristleworms. Over time, they have been known to reduce numbers of pesky bristleworms in home aquariums that are overrun. Dottybacks will eat most popular aquarium foods including a variety of frozen foods and Easy Reefs DKI pellets and Masstick. Feeding on C1, C2, Hikari Mysis, PE Calanus. This Dottyback is semi-aggressive and requires a 30 gallon aquarium when full grown. 

Proaquatix fish - Please choose a Friday Delivery

products.title: Panama Gorgonian - Antillogorgia
products.body_html:
Antillogorgia sp. This variety of Gorgonian was cultured in Panama at Bocas Mariculture. It has purple branches with small polyps. Large colonies are impressive with their hypnotic movements and many fuzzy branches. Most are purple, some more rare are yellow. 

Many other species of gorgonians and sea fans are difficult to keep in captivity, however, this species is relatively easy to keep and a great coral for beginners who want something with interesting form and flow. It is photosynthetic, but will benefit from feedings of microfoods designed for soft corals.

products.title: Panama Gorgonian Eunicea
products.body_html:
Eunicea sp. This variety of Gorgonian was cultured in Panama at Bocas Mariculture. It has thin, purple, elegant branches with large polyps. Large colonies are impressive with their hypnotic movements and many fuzzy branches.

Many other species of gorgonians and sea fans are difficult to keep in captivity, however, this species is relatively easy to keep and a great coral for beginners who want something with interesting form and flow. It is photosynthetic, but will benefit from feedings of microfoods designed for soft corals.

products.title: Purple Zebra Porcelain Crab
products.body_html:
Petrolisthes bolivarensis - The Purple Zebra Porcelain Crab is a seldom seen, reef safe "crab" perfect for nano tanks. They're extremely peaceful and don't harm any other fish or invertebrates. They can be kept with the smallest of gobies and even seahorses. 

This species can be identified from other similar species by the orange spots on its claws and blue mouth. 

It uses two "feathery baskets" at the end of its feeding arms (third maxillipeds) to filter food from the water column. It will also accept bits of prepared foods like tiny pellets and frozen foods.

Biota recommends introducing new livestock using an acclimation box. These animals are small on arrival and tend to hide very well.

products.title: Tessellated Blenny
products.body_html:
Hypsoblennius invemar - The Tessellated Blenny is very rare in the aquarium trade. Not only are they stunningly beautiful with their bright red spots, they also tend to have adorable personalities that endear them to their humans. Like many other blennies, they tend to camouflage and change color depending on their mood and the environment. Some aquarists report that when they are happy or excited, the red coloration intensifies. 

In the wild they like to live in empty barnacle clusters, but they'll also inhabit holes in rockwork or decorations. Their behavior is similar to other barnacle blennies. They only reach a couple inches in length, so they make great fish for nano tanks. This species is generally quite peaceful with other fish species but can become aggressive toward similar species. 

This omnivorous species does well on a varied diet of pellet foods and frozen foods like small Mysis, Spirulina brine shrimp, and Calanus. They aren't picky eaters and will try most prepared aquarium foods and will even nibble on algae in the aquarium. The addition of Easy Reefs DKI β-carotene Fish Care pellet food to their diet can help intensify and maintain their bright red coloration. 

Not much is known about keeping this species in reef aquariums, but they're likely as reef safe as other similar species. They have been safely kept with with Caribbean Zoas, corallimorphs, soft and stony corals.

These fish are small on arrival, so we recommend to use an acclimation box to introduce them to a tank, especially a larger tank or tanks with larger tank mates.

products.title: Discosoma Mushroom Red/Orange assorted
products.body_html:
Red Discosoma mushrooms are easy to keep and a great choice for beginners stocking a newly matured aquarium. These specimens are cultured and not taken from the reef. 

Mushroom corals need low to moderate lighting and water flow. Be sure to acclimate new coral to bright lighting slowly and carefully.

products.title: Lemonpeel Angelfish
products.body_html: Centropyge flavissima - The lemon peel angelfish is one of the most underrated dwarf angelfish in the industry. Sporting some of the brightest yellow coloration with light blue accents around the eyes, gills, and sometimes lips this fish is sure to brighten up any aquarium. They stay relatively small with a 5” max sizing but tend to stay a bit smaller in home aquariums. We recommend using an acclimation box to introduce all new fish to the aquarium. They are cautiously reef safe if they are consistently well fed a varied diet and monitor any coral picking behavior closely.

products.title: Green Frogspawn
products.body_html:
Fimbriaphyllia divisa - This Biota Green Frogspawn colony is sought after for its interesting coloration and flowing tentacles. These corals are cultured and not taken from the reef. 

Give plenty of space for this stinging coral to grow away from other species. Torch corals need moderate lighting and moderate water flow. It's necessary to maintain stable alkalinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace elements. Be sure to acclimate new coral to bright lighting slowly and carefully.

products.body_html:
